turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've just been feeling really crummy for the past two weeks. I started with a dry cough that was annoying me, but it's turned into this persistent, productive cough and I'm having trouble breathing too. It's getting worse at night and it's making me really tired all the time.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: Yeah, it's been driving me crazy! I've tried taking over-the-counter cough medicine, but it doesn't seem to be doing much to help.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I get a little feverish sometimes, usually around 38.2 degrees Celsius, but it's not super high and it comes and goes. And I've also been getting night sweats, which is really weird because I don't normally sweat that much at night.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: No, I don't usually feel like I'm going to pass out or anything, and I haven't noticed any excessive sweating except for at night. But to be honest, it's kind of hard to get comfortable at all when my chest feels so tight and wheezy... that's a big part of why I've been having trouble sleeping.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: The discomfort is mostly in my chest, but sometimes it feels like it radiates to my back when I'm coughing or taking deep breaths. It's a sharp pain, kind of stabbing feeling that catches me off guard.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah... sometimes it feels like my throat is sore too, especially after coughing or trying to swallow something. And I've also been feeling pretty fatigued all over, not just in my chest and back. It's like my whole body is just tired and drained of energy.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: To be honest, no, I haven't noticed any swelling at all. But now that you mention it, I did have a bit of a sore throat last week and my partner mentioned that I looked a little pale when I was feeling particularly tired one day.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: I haven't really had any nausea or stomach issues, but I've definitely lost my appetite over the past few days. Even thinking about food makes me feel a bit queasy, which is unusual for me because I usually love cooking and trying new recipes.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy's been really low, like I mentioned earlier. I've had to miss three days of work already and even simple tasks feel exhausting. And yeah, my appetite's definitely decreased - I'm not really hungry at all, which is weird for me because I usually love food.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've already told you about my feverish episodes and the night sweats. The fever is usually around 38.2 degrees Celsius and comes and goes. And yeah, the night sweats have been pretty consistent for a few nights now. It's just really weird because I don't normally sweat that much at night.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: Well, I have seasonal allergies, which I'm sure you can relate to living in a place like Portland where it's always... damp and green, right? Anyway, that's pretty much it. I take cetirizine when my allergies act up, but this coughing thing is totally unrelated to that. And no, I've never smoked or used any recreational substances. Just a normal, healthy-ish person who's been feeling really unwell lately!

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's just been hard for me to remember to take my cetirizine regularly when my allergies aren't acting up. And with this cough going on, I've kind of stopped taking it altogether because I didn't think it would help with this respiratory thing. But I do have a bottle at home if you want to check it out!

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: I try to eat healthy most of the time, but lately, my appetite has been so low that even thinking about food is exhausting. Usually, I'd have oatmeal with fruit for breakfast, a sandwich or leftovers for lunch at work, and maybe some stir-fry or roasted vegetables for dinner with my partner. But now, I'm just not hungry at all. It's really weird because cooking and trying new recipes are things I usually enjoy.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, exercising has been pretty much impossible for me lately. Just walking around the apartment is exhausting, and I haven't been able to do any of my usual activities like hiking or yoga because even simple movements make me feel winded. It's really frustrating because I know how important it is to stay active, but right now, just getting through each day feels like a challenge in itself.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: You asked me about my mom earlier, and I didn't mention her asthma. Yeah, she's been managing it with medication for years, but that's all I know. And actually, my maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer when I was a kid, so there might be some concern there too...

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: It's weird... ever since my grandmother passed away from lung cancer, I've always had a tiny voice in the back of my head worrying about my own lungs and whether I'll develop something like that too. But I try not to think about it too much because it just makes me anxious. And now with this persistent cough and trouble breathing... yeah, it's definitely brought up some old fears for me.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I drink socially, just a glass or two of wine on weekends, nothing too excessive. As for coping with stress, I try to exercise, but like I said, that's been really hard lately. When I'm feeling overwhelmed, I usually just try to take some time for myself, whether it's reading a book or taking a warm bath. But to be honest, this whole experience has been really stressful and anxiety-provoking for me...

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke at all and I've never used any recreational substances. Like I said earlier, I'm a pretty normal, healthy-ish person who's been feeling really unwell lately!

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't mentioned my job much yet, but I work with young children all day at an elementary school. I'm not sure if that's relevant, but I just thought it might be worth mentioning since we talked about how contagious this cough has been and stuff.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been a little hesitant to mention my job because I'm worried it might make things worse for me or for the kids at school. But now that you bring it up, I think it's probably relevant. I just hope we can figure out what's going on with this cough so I can get back to being able to teach and interact with the kids without worrying about spreading my illness to them.
